Understanding Tidal Energy

Tidal energy, unlike wind or solar power, draws on one of the most predictable forces in nature: the gravitational pull of the moon and sun on the oceans. Engineers have chased this predictability for decades, building turbines that sit on the sea floor and spin as tides move past them twice a day, every day, on a schedule that can be calculated centuries in advance.

The appeal is obvious on paper. A tidal turbine does not depend on weather forecasts the way a solar panel or a wind turbine does. But the ocean environment is unforgiving. Saltwater corrodes metal quickly, marine growth fouls moving parts, and storms capable of damaging offshore equipment are common in many of the coastal regions best suited for tidal power. Projects that looked economical on a spreadsheet have often struggled once real maintenance costs arrived.

Even so, several demonstration projects around the world have now run for years without major failure, proving that the basic engineering problem is solvable. The remaining question is economic rather than technical: whether tidal power can ever compete on cost with solar and wind installations that have benefited from a much larger, much faster scaling curve over the last two decades.
